Go Syntax.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Go Syntax.

lessons.title: Hello Go
lessons.theory:
## Welcome to Go
Go (Golang) is a statically typed, compiled language designed at Google.

```go
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}
```

- `package main`: Tells the compiler this is an executable
- `import "fmt"`: Imports the formatting package (I/O)
- `func main()`: The entry point of the program
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Your First Go Program
1. Write a Go program that prints a greeting
2. The package must be `main`
3. Use `fmt.Println` to output "Hello from Go!"
lessons.hint: package main. import "fmt". func main(). fmt.Println.
lessons.rubric: Code matches Go structure and prints 'Hello from Go!'

lessons.title: Variables & Types
lessons.theory:
## Declaring Variables
Go has explicit types but also supports type inference.

```go
// Explicit typing
var age int = 25
var name string = "Alice"

// Type inference
var score = 100

// Short declaration (inside functions only)
city := "New York"
pi := 3.14
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Declare Variables
1. Declare `year` as an int using explicit typing
2. Declare `language` as a string using type inference (`var`)
3. Declare `version` as a float using short declaration (`:=`)
4. Print them all
lessons.starterCode:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    ___ year ___ = 2024
    ___ language = "Go"
    version ___ 1.22
    
    fmt.Printf("Year: %d, Language: %s, Version: %.2f\n", year, language, version)
}
lessons.solution:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    var year int = 2024
    var language = "Go"
    version := 1.22
    
    fmt.Printf("Year: %d, Language: %s, Version: %.2f\n", year, language, version)
}
lessons.hint: var name type. var name = value. name := value.
lessons.rubric: All three variable types declared correctly and printed.

lessons.title: Functions in Go
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Math Functions
1. Write `max(a, b int) int` that returns the larger number
2. Write `swap(x, y string) (string, string)` that returns both strings swapped
3. Test both in main
lessons.hint: Specify types for params and returns. Return y, x to swap.
lessons.rubric: Max returns 20. Swap returns 'second first'.

Structs & Interfaces.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Structs & Interfaces.

lessons.title: Object-Oriented Go?
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Implement an Interface
1. Create a `Circle` struct.
2. Implement the `Shape` interface's `Area` method.
lessons.hint: The method receiver needs the struct name 'Circle'.
lessons.rubric: Go pseudocode demonstrates a proper method receiver for the interface.

Goroutines.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Goroutines.

lessons.title: Goroutines Basics
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Start Goroutines
1. Write a function `printNumbers(prefix string)` that prints 1 to 3
2. Call it once normally, and once as a goroutine
3. Simulate a small delay with `time.Sleep` in the loop
lessons.hint: Prefix function call with 'go' to start a goroutine.
lessons.rubric: Both Sync and Async print 1 to 3, likely interleaved.

lessons.title: Channels
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Channel Sender & Receiver
1. Create a channel of strings
2. Start a goroutine that sends 3 messages to the channel
3. Receive and print the 3 messages in main
lessons.hint: make(chan type). ch <- val sends. val := <-ch receives.
lessons.rubric: Channel created. Messages sent with <- and received with <-. Prints Ping Pong Done.

Go Web Servers.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Go Web Servers.

lessons.title: net/http
lessons.theory:
## Built-in Web Servers
Go's standard library `net/http` is incredibly powerful. You often don't even need a framework to build a fast web server.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Simple HTTP Handler
1. Write a basic HTTP handler function that writes 'Hello Go' to the response.
lessons.hint: Use 'w' for ResponseWriter and pass 'handler' to HandleFunc.
lessons.rubric: Go pseudocode correctly defines a basic net/http handler.

Docker Containers.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Docker Containers.

lessons.title: What is Docker?
lessons.theory:
## Containers
Docker packages applications with all their dependencies into standardized units called **containers**.

Benefits:
- "It works on my machine" -> "It works everywhere"
- Lightweight compared to virtual machines
- Fast startup
- Isolated environments

A **Dockerfile** is the blueprint for creating a container image.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Docker Commands
1. Create a dictionary matching Docker commands to their functions
2. Include: build, run, ps, stop, rmi
3. Print them out
lessons.hint: build creates images. run starts containers. ps lists them. rmi removes images.
lessons.rubric: All 5 Docker commands correctly identified.

lessons.title: Writing a Dockerfile
lessons.theory:
## Anatomy of a Dockerfile

```dockerfile
# Base image
FROM python:3.9-slim

# Working directory inside container
WORKDIR /app

# Copy files
COPY requirements.txt .

# Run commands during build
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt

# Copy rest of the code
COPY . .

# Command to run when container starts
CMD ["python", "app.py"]
```
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Node.js Dockerfile
1. Write the text of a Dockerfile for a Node.js app
2. Use `node:18-alpine` as base
3. Copy package.json, run `npm install`, copy code, run `npm start`
lessons.starterCode:
dockerfile = """
___ node:18-alpine

___ /usr/src/app

___ package*.json ./

___ npm install

___ . .

___ ["npm", "start"]
"""

print("Your Dockerfile:")
print(dockerfile)
lessons.solution:
dockerfile = """
FROM node:18-alpine

WORKDIR /usr/src/app

COPY package*.json ./

RUN npm install

COPY . .

CMD ["npm", "start"]
"""

print("Your Dockerfile:")
print(dockerfile)
lessons.hint: FROM sets base. WORKDIR sets dir. COPY moves files. RUN executes in build. CMD is startup.
lessons.rubric: Valid Node.js Dockerfile using standard keywords.

Kubernetes Basics.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Kubernetes Basics.

lessons.title: Orchestrating Containers
lessons.theory:
## Managing Docker at Scale
If you have 1 container, use Docker. If you have 10,000 containers across 50 servers, use Kubernetes (K8s).

- **Pod**: The smallest unit in K8s (usually contains 1 container).
- **Deployment**: Manages Pods (e.g., "Make sure exactly 3 copies of my API pod are always running").
- **Service**: Gives a stable IP address so other parts of the app can talk to the Pods.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: YAML Matcher
1. Match the K8s object type to its role.
2. Roles: 'Pod', 'Deployment', 'Service'
lessons.hint: Service for Network. Deployment for Replicas. Pod for Container.
lessons.rubric: Objects correctly mapped to their roles.

gRPC.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for gRPC.

lessons.title: High-Speed APIs
lessons.theory:
## Faster than REST
REST uses HTTP/1.1 and JSON. JSON is heavy and slow to parse.

**gRPC** (developed by Google) uses HTTP/2 and **Protocol Buffers** (Protobuf). Protobufs are binary, making them extremely fast and compact.

With gRPC, you define your service in a `.proto` file, and it automatically generates the server and client code for you in almost any language (Go, Python, Java, etc).
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Protobuf vs JSON Size\n1. Compare the string length of a JSON object vs a mock Binary representation.\n2. Print the difference in size.
lessons.starterCode:
import json

data = {"id": 104, "name": "Alice", "active": True}

# Standard REST JSON string
json_string = json.___(data)
json_size = len(json_string)

# Mock gRPC Protobuf binary (usually much smaller, just the raw data packed)
mock_binary_string = "104Alice1"
binary_size = len(___)

print(f"JSON Size: {json_size} bytes")
print(f"Protobuf Size: {binary_size} bytes")
print(f"gRPC is {json_size - binary_size} bytes smaller!")
lessons.solution:
import json

data = {"id": 104, "name": "Alice", "active": True}

# Standard REST JSON string
json_string = json.dumps(data)
json_size = len(json_string)

# Mock gRPC Protobuf binary (usually much smaller, just the raw data packed)
mock_binary_string = "104Alice1"
binary_size = len(mock_binary_string)

print(f"JSON Size: {json_size} bytes")
print(f"Protobuf Size: {binary_size} bytes")
print(f"gRPC is {json_size - binary_size} bytes smaller!")
lessons.hint: Use json.dumps(). Check length of mock_binary_string.
lessons.rubric: Shows JSON is larger (around 40 bytes) compared to the mock binary (9 bytes).

Service Meshes.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Service Meshes.

lessons.title: Managing Microservices
lessons.theory:
## Istio & Linkerd
When you have 50 microservices talking to each other, how do you handle security, retries, and monitoring? Doing it in the code is messy.

A **Service Mesh** injects a 'Sidecar Proxy' next to every single service. When Service A wants to talk to Service B, it actually talks to its own proxy, which securely routes the traffic to B's proxy.

This allows you to enforce mTLS (mutual encryption) without writing a single line of application code.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Sidecar Simulator\n1. Simulate a request going through a sidecar proxy.\n2. The proxy should intercept the request, encrypt it, and pass it to the destination.
lessons.starterCode:
class SidecarProxy:
    def __init__(self, service_name):
        self.service_name = service_name
        
    def send(self, destination, data):
        print(f"[{self.service_name} Proxy] Encrypting data...")
        encrypted = f"**{data}**"
        print(f"[{self.service_name} Proxy] Sending to {destination.service_name} Proxy...")
        destination.___(encrypted)
        
    def receive(self, data):
        print(f"[{self.service_name} Proxy] Decrypting data...")
        decrypted = data.replace('*', '')
        print(f"[{self.service_name} App] Received: {decrypted}")

app_a = SidecarProxy("Service_A")
app_b = SidecarProxy("Service_B")

app_a.___(app_b, "Hello Microservice!")
lessons.solution:
class SidecarProxy:
    def __init__(self, service_name):
        self.service_name = service_name
        
    def send(self, destination, data):
        print(f"[{self.service_name} Proxy] Encrypting data...")
        encrypted = f"**{data}**"
        print(f"[{self.service_name} Proxy] Sending to {destination.service_name} Proxy...")
        destination.receive(encrypted)
        
    def receive(self, data):
        print(f"[{self.service_name} Proxy] Decrypting data...")
        decrypted = data.replace('*', '')
        print(f"[{self.service_name} App] Received: {decrypted}")

app_a = SidecarProxy("Service_A")
app_b = SidecarProxy("Service_B")

app_a.send(app_b, "Hello Microservice!")
lessons.hint: destination.receive(encrypted) and app_a.send(app_b, ...)
lessons.rubric: Data successfully routes A Proxy -> B Proxy -> B App.

Cloud Operators.aiRubric: Check logic, syntax, and output for Cloud Operators.

lessons.title: K8s Operators
lessons.theory:
## Automating Kubernetes
An Operator is a software extension to Kubernetes that makes use of custom resources to manage applications and their components.

Instead of a human reading a manual on how to backup a database, you write a K8s Operator that automatically knows how to backup, restore, and scale your specific database.
lessons.instructions:
## Task: Operator Loop
1. An operator constantly runs a 'Reconciliation Loop'.
2. It checks the 'Desired State' against the 'Current State'.
3. Simulate an operator checking if a database has a backup.
lessons.starterCode:
def reconcile_loop(current_state, desired_state):
    print("--- Reconciliation Loop Running ---")
    
    if current_state['needs_backup'] != desired_state['___']:
        print("[Operator Action] Taking database backup to match desired state...")
        current_state['___'] = desired_state['___']
        return "Action Taken"
    
    return "No Action Needed"

desired = {'needs_backup': False} # We don't want it to NEED a backup, we want it DONE
current = {'needs_backup': True}

print(reconcile_loop(current, desired))
# Loop runs again later
print(reconcile_loop(current, desired))
lessons.solution:
def reconcile_loop(current_state, desired_state):
    print("--- Reconciliation Loop Running ---")
    
    if current_state['needs_backup'] != desired_state['needs_backup']:
        print("[Operator Action] Taking database backup to match desired state...")
        current_state['needs_backup'] = desired_state['needs_backup']
        return "Action Taken"
    
    return "No Action Needed"

desired = {'needs_backup': False} # We don't want it to NEED a backup, we want it DONE
current = {'needs_backup': True}

print(reconcile_loop(current, desired))
# Loop runs again later
print(reconcile_loop(current, desired))
lessons.hint: Key is 'needs_backup'.
lessons.rubric: First loop takes action, second loop takes no action.

lessons.title: Go Mod Init
lessons.theory: ## Dependency Management\nGo modules are the standard way to manage dependencies in Go projects. The `go mod init` command initializes a new module in your project directory.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Init a Module\nWrite the command to initialize a new module named 'github.com/mabel/app'.
lessons.starterCode: go ___ ___ github.com/mabel/app
lessons.solution: go mod init github.com/mabel/app
lessons.hint: Use mod init
lessons.rubric: Correctly constructs the go mod init command.

lessons.title: Importing Packages
lessons.theory: ## Standard Library\nGo comes with a rich standard library. You use the `import` keyword to bring packages into your program.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Import Formatting\nImport the `fmt` package and use it to print 'Hello'.
lessons.hint: Import fmt and use Println
lessons.rubric: Correctly imports fmt and calls Println.

lessons.title: Channel Basics
lessons.theory: ## Safe Communication\nChannels are the pipes that connect concurrent goroutines. You can send values into channels from one goroutine and receive those values into another.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Send and Receive\nCreate a channel of integers, send the number 42 into it, and receive it.
lessons.hint: Use make(chan int) and the <- operator
lessons.rubric: Correctly uses make(chan int) and the send/receive operators.

lessons.title: The Select Statement
lessons.theory: ## Multiplexing\nThe `select` statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple communication operations. It blocks until one of its cases can run.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Select on Two Channels\nWrite a select statement that waits for a message from `ch1` or `ch2`.
lessons.hint: Use <- operator and case keyword
lessons.rubric: Correctly uses the case keyword and receive operator.

lessons.title: The Package Manager
lessons.theory: ## Kubernetes Templates\nHelm is the package manager for Kubernetes. A Helm 'Chart' is a collection of files that describe a related set of Kubernetes resources.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Install a Chart\nWrite the helm command to install a chart named 'my-redis' from the bitnami repository.
lessons.starterCode: helm ___ my-redis bitnami/___
lessons.solution: helm install my-redis bitnami/redis
lessons.hint: Use install and redis
lessons.rubric: Correctly uses the helm install command.

lessons.title: Values injected in Templates
lessons.theory: ## Customizing Deployments\nHelm templates use the Go template engine. You can pass a `values.yaml` file to customize the Kubernetes manifests before applying.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Accessing Values\nWrite the template syntax to inject the `replicaCount` from the values file.
lessons.starterCode: spec:\n  replicas: {{ .___.___ }}
lessons.solution: spec:\n  replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }}
lessons.hint: Use .Values.replicaCount
lessons.rubric: Correctly references .Values.replicaCount.

lessons.title: Custom Resource Definitions
lessons.theory: ## Extending the API\nA Custom Resource Definition (CRD) allows you to define your own object types in the Kubernetes API, just like Pods or Deployments.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Define a CRD Group\nSpecify the `group` and `version` for a custom resource named `Database`.
lessons.starterCode:
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: databases.mabel.io
spec:
  group: ___
  versions:
    - name: ___
lessons.solution:
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: databases.mabel.io
spec:
  group: mabel.io
  versions:
    - name: v1
lessons.hint: Use mabel.io and v1
lessons.rubric: Correctly extracts the group from the name and sets version v1.

lessons.title: Reconciliation Loop
lessons.theory: ## The Control Loop\nAn Operator contains a custom controller that watches your CRDs. Its core logic is the 'Reconcile' loop, which constantly ensures the current state matches the desired state.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Return Result\nIn a Kubebuilder Reconcile function, return a successful empty result so the loop knows it finished correctly.
lessons.hint: Return empty struct {} and nil error
lessons.rubric: Correctly returns ctrl.Result{} and nil.

lessons.title: Traffic Shifting
lessons.theory: ## Canary Deployments\nIstio allows you to split traffic between different versions of a service seamlessly, which is perfect for A/B testing or Canary releases.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: Virtual Service Weights\nConfigure an Istio VirtualService to send 90% of traffic to v1 and 10% to v2.
lessons.starterCode:
route:
- destination:
    host: my-service
    subset: v1
  weight: ___
- destination:
    host: my-service
    subset: v2
  weight: ___
lessons.solution:
route:
- destination:
    host: my-service
    subset: v1
  weight: 90
- destination:
    host: my-service
    subset: v2
  weight: 10
lessons.hint: Use 90 and 10
lessons.rubric: Correctly sets the weights to 90 and 10.

lessons.title: Mutual TLS (mTLS)
lessons.theory: ## Secure Communication\nIstio can automatically encrypt all traffic between microservices in your cluster using mTLS, without changing any application code.
lessons.instructions: ## Task: PeerAuthentication\nSet the mTLS mode to STRICT for the entire 'default' namespace.
lessons.starterCode:
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: default
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: ___
lessons.solution:
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: default
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT
lessons.hint: Use STRICT
lessons.rubric: Sets mTLS mode to STRICT.
